suggested.title: [Activity] "Tell me about your day" with standards

competence_markers.description: The child speaks in complete sentences as a default in substantive communication
The child can introduce themselves to an unfamiliar adult with clear voice, eye contact, and a complete greeting
The child can order food in a restaurant and ask for help in a store without parental intermediary
The child speaks with sufficient volume to be heard clearly by the listener, adjusting for the environment
The child answers questions with substance — at least two sentences with detail, not one-word responses
The child can give clear, sequential instructions for a simple task that a listener can follow
The child demonstrates growing vocabulary through natural use of new words in conversation
The child engages with narration prompts with detail and personal connection, not minimal compliance

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the most interesting thing that happened to you this week.
narration_prompts.detail: Give me enough detail that I can picture it.

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain how to do something you know how to do well
narration_prompts.detail: — make a bed, build with blocks, play a game. Give me every step, in order, clearly enough that I could do it without seeing you.

narration_prompts.prompt: What new word did you learn recently?
narration_prompts.detail: What does it mean? Use it in a sentence for me.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you talked to an adult who was not your parent this week.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you say? How did they respond? Was it easy or hard?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between saying "I'm mad" and "I'm frustrated"?
narration_prompts.detail: When would you use one and not the other? Can you think of other words that seem the same but are really a little different?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why does it matter to speak in complete sentences?
narration_prompts.detail: What happens when you do not?

cross_connections.description: Vocabulary, grammar, and sentence structure provide the raw material for verbal communication. The child building language literacy builds verbal communication simultaneously.
Clear writing and clear speaking develop the same mental habits — organizing thoughts, choosing precise words, structuring ideas logically. Read-aloud and narration build both.
Verbal clarity needs physical backing. The child who speaks clearly but mumbles into the floor is undermining their own words. These two sub-domains develop together.
The verbal clarity developed here is the engine that drives conversation. A child who can speak in complete sentences and answer questions fully can sustain a conversation.
The discipline to form thoughts into complete sentences before speaking — rather than blurting fragments — is a self-regulation skill rooted in IC1.
Giving clear instructions and explaining processes sequentially builds the same logical thinking developed in numeracy.

content.why_this_matters:
You have thoughts in your head all day long. Ideas, questions, wants, feelings, stories. But those thoughts do not help anyone — including you — unless you can get them out of your head and into the world clearly enough that other people understand them.

That is what verbal communication is: saying what you mean so that the person listening actually understands you. Not mumbling. Not pointing and grunting. Not saying "that thing" when you mean the specific thing. Not answering "fine" when someone asks how your day was and it was actually really interesting.

The child who learns to speak clearly, in complete sentences, with enough volume that people can hear and enough detail that people can understand — that child has a power that stays with them forever. They can order their own food. They can ask a librarian for help. They can explain to a doctor where it hurts. They can tell a friend what happened at the park. They can introduce themselves to anyone.

This is not about being fancy or using big words. It is about being clear. Clarity is a skill. You are going to build it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Speaking in complete sentences** — the habit of articulating full thoughts, not fragments or gestures
2. **Vocabulary precision** — using the right word for what you mean, not an approximate one
3. **Addressing adults with confidence** — speaking directly to unfamiliar adults without hiding behind a parent
4. **Volume and projection** — matching voice volume to the situation and being heard without shouting
5. **Answering questions fully** — responding with substance, not one-word answers
6. **Giving clear instructions** — explaining how to do something in sequential, understandable steps
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Complete Sentences — The First Rule

A complete sentence has a subject (who or what) and a verb (what happens). "I went to the store." "The dog ate his dinner." "We played in the garden."

Why does this matter? Because fragments leave the listener guessing. If someone asks "What do you want?" and you point at something across the room, the person has to figure out what you are pointing at, which thing on the shelf, and what you want them to do about it. But if you say "May I have the blue cup on the top shelf, please?" — there is no guessing. The communication is complete.

Starting now, you are going to speak in complete sentences as your default. Not every single time — casual conversation allows shortcuts. But when you are speaking to adults, answering questions, giving instructions, or telling a story, complete sentences are the standard.

**Practice:** For one full day, try to notice every time you use a fragment or a gesture instead of words. How many times do you point instead of describing? How many times do you say "that" or "it" when you could say the specific thing you mean?

### Part 2: The Right Word

"I'm mad" and "I'm frustrated" describe different feelings. "I'm sad" and "I'm disappointed" describe different feelings. "Big" and "enormous" describe different sizes. The more words you know, and the more precisely you use them, the more clearly people understand you.

This does not mean using big words to sound smart. It means using the right word for what you actually mean. If you are not angry but you are annoyed, saying "I'm annoyed" communicates more accurately than "I'm mad." If something is not just good but fascinating, saying "fascinating" tells the listener exactly how good.

You build vocabulary by reading, by listening, and by using new words as soon as you learn them. When you hear a word you do not know, ask what it means. When you learn what it means, use it in a sentence that day. Words you use become words you own.

**Practice:** This week, learn three new words from your reading or conversations. Use each one in a real conversation at least once.

### Part 3: Talking to Adults

Here is something many children are never taught: you can talk to adults. Not just your parents. Any adult. The server at a restaurant. The librarian. Your parents' friends. Your teacher. The person behind the counter at a store.

You do not need your parent to speak for you. You can walk up to a librarian and say, "Excuse me, I am looking for a book about dinosaurs. Can you help me?" You can tell a server, "I would like the grilled cheese, please." You can meet your parents' friend and say, "Hello, I'm [name]. Nice to meet you."

The more you do this, the easier it gets. The child who has spoken directly to a hundred different adults by age eight is comfortable talking to anyone. The child who has always had a parent speak for them is not.

**Practice:** This week, speak directly to at least three adults who are not your parents: order your own food, ask a store employee a question, or introduce yourself to someone new. Do it without anyone prompting you.

### Part 4: Volume — Being Heard

Speaking clearly means nothing if no one can hear you. Volume is not about being loud. It is about matching your voice to the situation.

**In a conversation:** Your voice should be loud enough that the person you are talking to can hear you without leaning in or asking you to repeat yourself.

**In a group:** Your voice should reach the person farthest from you. This requires projecting — speaking from your diaphragm (your belly), not just your throat.

**In a quiet place** (a library, a church, someone's house during naptime): Your voice should be quiet enough not to disturb others.

The most common problem is mumbling — speaking so quietly that people cannot hear you. If someone asks you to repeat yourself more than once, your volume is too low. The fix is not shouting. It is speaking from your belly with your mouth open enough to form the words clearly.

**Practice:** Read a short passage out loud to someone standing across the room. Can they hear every word? If not, increase your volume without shouting. Practice until you can project across a room clearly and naturally.

### Part 5: Answering Questions Like You Mean It

"How was school?" "Fine."
"What did you do today?" "Nothing."
"Did you like the book?" "Yeah."

These answers are technically correct and completely useless. They give the other person nothing to work with. They close the conversation instead of opening it.

A good answer gives substance. "School was fun today — we did an experiment with magnets in science, and I got to show the class how they attract and repel." That answer is specific, interesting, and gives the other person something to ask about next.

You are not required to give a long speech every time someone asks you a question. But you are required to give enough information that the person who asked actually learns something.

**Practice:** For one week, when someone asks you a question, give at least two sentences in your answer. "How was your day?" gets a real answer with detail. "Did you like the movie?" gets a reason.

### Part 6: Giving Clear Instructions

Here is a test: explain to someone how to make a peanut butter sandwich. Only using words. They can only do exactly what you say.

If you say "Put the peanut butter on the bread," they might put the entire jar on an unsliced loaf. You need to say: "Take two slices of bread out of the bag. Pick up the knife. Open the jar of peanut butter. Scoop some peanut butter onto the knife. Spread it across one piece of bread..."

Giving clear instructions requires you to think through every step and say them in order. This is harder than it sounds, and it is one of the most useful communication skills you will ever develop. The person who can give clear instructions can teach, can lead, can explain, and can help.

**Practice:** Pick a simple task you know how to do (tie a shoe, fold a shirt, draw a star). Give instructions to someone who follows them exactly as stated. If they make a mistake, the instructions were unclear. Revise and try again.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Complete Sentence Standard [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
The mentor maintains a consistent expectation of complete sentences in all substantive communication. When the child uses fragments or gestures instead of words, the mentor says "Can you say that in a full sentence?" This is not nagging — it is calibrating the standard. Over weeks, complete sentences become the default.

### Study 2: The Restaurant/Store Challenge [Ongoing]
Every opportunity to interact with a service worker is a practice opportunity. The child orders food, asks for help in stores, checks out library books, and introduces themselves — all without parental intermediary. The mentor facilitates opportunities but steps back to let the child speak.

### Study 3: The Instruction Game [Monthly]
The child gives verbal instructions for a simple task. The listener follows the instructions literally — if the instructions are vague, the result is wrong (and often funny). This makes the gap between what the child said and what they meant visible and concrete.

### Study 4: Vocabulary Building Through Reading [Ongoing]
During read-aloud time, new vocabulary words are noted and discussed. The child uses at least one new word from each reading session in conversation that day. Over months, this builds a rich, precise vocabulary through natural exposure rather than drill.

### Study 5: The "Tell Me About" Practice [Ongoing]
Daily narration of the day's events, a book, a movie, or an experience — in complete sentences, with detail, with enough information that the listener can picture what happened. This builds verbal fluency and the habit of organized expression.

### Study 6: Volume Check [Early Packet]
The child reads a passage aloud to someone across the room and adjusts volume until every word is clear. Practice with different room sizes. The goal is natural projection — audible without shouting.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Complete Sentences
**Developing well:** The child speaks in full sentences in most substantive conversations without reminders. Fragments are the exception, not the rule.
**Developing slowly:** The child still defaults to fragments, gestures, and one-word responses despite consistent reminders.
**Diagnostic:** Are you modeling complete sentences consistently? Is the expectation present in every interaction, or only during "language time"?

### 2. Speaking to Adults
**Developing well:** The child confidently approaches unfamiliar adults, speaks at appropriate volume, and completes the interaction independently.
**Developing slowly:** The child hides behind the parent, whispers, or refuses to speak to unfamiliar adults.
**Diagnostic:** Is the child getting enough practice? Frequency matters. One interaction per week is not enough. Aim for daily.

### 3. Volume and Projection
**Developing well:** The child can be heard clearly in conversation and can project across a room when needed.
**Developing slowly:** The child mumbles habitually despite reminders.
**Diagnostic:** Is the child anxious or simply unaware? Anxious children need the frequency of practice increased gradually. Unaware children need immediate feedback: "I couldn't hear that — can you say it again louder?"

### 4. Answering Questions
**Developing well:** The child gives substantive, multi-sentence answers with specific detail.
**Developing slowly:** One-word answers remain the default. The child answers "fine" to "how was your day?" despite repeated requests for detail.
**Diagnostic:** Are your questions specific enough? "How was your day?" invites "fine." "What was the most interesting thing that happened today?" invites detail.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Speaking for the Child
The most common and most damaging pitfall. When the server asks the child what they want, the parent answers. When a friend says hello to the child, the parent responds. Every time you speak for the child, you steal a practice repetition and reinforce the idea that they cannot handle the interaction.

### Accepting "Fine"
"Fine" is not an answer. It is a deflection. Gently refuse it: "I know you can tell me more than that. What actually happened?" The child who learns that "fine" is accepted will use it for the next twenty years.

### Making It a Performance
This should not feel like speech class. The standard is woven into daily life, not imposed as a separate activity. The child practices by living normally — with higher expectations for how they communicate.
